description: English Ivy (Hedera helix) is a common climbing vine toxic to cats. It contains triterpenoid saponins (including hederagenin and alpha-hederin) and polyacetylene compounds (falcarinol) throughout its leaves and berries. These compounds cause gastrointestinal irritation and, with repeated skin contact, dermatitis in sensitive individuals.
name: English Ivy
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: triterpenoid saponins (hederagenin, alpha-hederin), polyacetylene compound falcarinol
Mechanism: saponins disrupt GI mucosal integrity; falcarinol is a contact irritant that can also cause systemic upset
Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, abdominal pain; large ingestion may cause ataxia or tremors
Berries are slightly more concentrated than leaves; all parts should be considered hazardous
Remove English Ivy from indoor spaces if you have cats; outdoor access to ivy-covered walls should also be restricted

symptoms.name: Vomiting
symptoms.onset: Acute (Symptoms typically appear shortly after ingestion,
